speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: You were going to say something just a moment ago. So what did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: When I retired, I'm very-- very active in sport. [INAUDIBLE] I was-- and I'm still active, I compete against people of my age. I belong to the-- they call it-- in the United States, they call it Masters. In-- in the world, they call it Veterans. And they-- every two years, they have world championship. And on two of the-- in one of the world championships, I won two second places.

speaker: interviewer
text: In-- in which sport?

speaker: interviewee
text: In track and field, in triple jump and the high jump. And then I-- I won American championship in-- in those in the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [INAUDIBLE] with five events. I won the first place. I had an American record for the triple jump for my age group for six years. And I won two, one first, one second, and one third in American championships in track and field in New York. And I enjoy it and I continue as long as I'm healthy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. Do you think your athleticism helped you survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it helped me survive because during the war when-- when I had to run, that helped.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I was shot at few times. I was in Warsaw. I was shot at twice in Warsaw. But I didn't get hit and I escaped. So life was-- was interesting. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did-- did you-- do you think of yourself as a religious man? Do you have any beliefs in God or anything like that that helps you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I say that-- that no one of us looks from the same angle. Everybody has his own interpretation of God. I have my own very private interpretation of God, in which I believe. I believe there's a force. I don't know which-- which form. I'm trying to-- to-- to grasp it, but I can't. But I'm trying. That something understands the whole thing. That something can happen from a-- from a rock that escaped from the Big Bang. If life can evolve from that-- people, thinking-- then there must be something that understands that whole thing. That is my-- that is my belief in God. But of religion, I think that most of the religions are doing a good job because everybody-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] everybody looks, as I said, from a different point at the image of God. And I think religion helps them to-- to communicate with their God. And perhaps keeps them on the side of goodness instead of the side of evil. Because there's such a thing like evil and-- and goodness. But since we are the only human-- since we human beings are--

speaker: interviewer
text: I have the gift of-- of-- of-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] of-- how can I say it? Gift of reasoning. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then I think it-- it up to us to either go on the side of evil or goodness. And it's only a little-- little-- maybe sometimes a little inconvenience.

speaker: interviewee
text: But it's very easy to-- to be on the side of evil because it's like during the war. I could have lived during the war very comfortably. I could have declared myself a German, and then-- but it was-- would be siding with the evil. I didn't have to help any Jew. But then again I would be one of the millions that stood by, which in my opinion, are as guilty as the one that committed those crimes. Because if those millions would say anything, at least would not support, then the thing would never happen.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's why I say come to believe in God. I think the people that believe in God who [? don't ?] analyzing him are the people that more likely will be on the side of goodness instead of evil. Why would a-- a peasant that has no philosophy-- the only philosophy-- his philosophy of God, that he thinks is a living thing-- and that person risked his life and his family to help a Jew? I know people like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: I know a man that-- a peasant without any education. Very simple man, very simple. But he dug a hole in his kitchen, [? with ?] his mother and-- and he. And in that hole, he was hiding a Jew with his two sons. They live now in-- in Schenectady. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They immigrated to the United States. They brought them to the United States, a very good friend. But the guidance that I have was to believe in God. So I think if someone believes in God is more likely to be-- to be a good person.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think, then, that's what helped you be a good person, that belief?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I just-- well, there wasn't such a thing in my family that-- that my father and mother said this is good, this is wrong. Well, we knew it. We knew it instinctive. My father was a very good man. Oh, the finest man I-- I-- I can think of. And we just know that you did something and something you didn't because it was wrong. No one had to teach us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. OK. Have you seen the movie Did you see the movie?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes, I saw that. Of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: There are some similarities between--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in my opinion, was the closest to the truth. and The other movie, I have some objection. Not completely, but let's take, for instance-- for instance showed the worse in-- in Polish people. The fellow went-- I don't know if you know that, but was funded by the Israeli government, supposed to be in two parts.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they went and [? read-- ?] and made the first part. But it was very very, very bad. The movie was so bad that the Israeli government said no more money. That's why it came all in one-- one-- one segment.

speaker: interviewee
text: But they shot people in Poland, they-- they must've looking for people like that. And they exist everywhere. You know that. Anti-semitism all over the world. Open or not open, but this, we have to-- to face it, and they probably found the worst guys in Poland in There was another one, was-- was Everything was fine till the moment when the showed the Polish cavalry helping the Germans to put the Jews in-- in-- in-- in the railroad car. There wasn't such a thing like a Polish army during that time because the Polish man was [INAUDIBLE]. But on top of it, those cavalry men had the same insignia that was my outfit had. They had black across, I don't know what you call it here. And blue and black, this little flag over here. So that was wrong. And-- and little things like that-- that-- that-- that nullify the whole thing. The same-- Dimont, for instance. Dimont wrote the book Everything was fine. I-- I learned a lot. But at one moment, he tells you how the Polish people helped to build the concentration camp. That-- that's nonsense. Nobody wanted to help the Germans and building concentration camp. And it was in the last pages, I think it was about concentration camp, and this was wrong. That's why I say if you deal with the situations, anti-semitism, then you have to tell the truth only. Up to the-- to the-- to the smallest detail. Otherwise, if someone exaggerate, it's wrong. If somebody doesn't say the whole thing is wrong too. If I would say there is no anti-semitism in Poland, I would-- that's wrong. I have to admit there is. There was. If somebody said there was ghettos in Poland, that's wrong. That the Jews could not go to universities, that's wrong.

speaker: interviewer
text: I heard from many Jews over the year that Jews could not go to the university in Poland, which was wrong. [? Your mother ?] knows that. Why would we have so many doctors and-- and-- and-- and lawyers in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Poland, we had, in-- in-- especially in-- in-- in literature-- the-- the-- the-- probably the greatest poet. For sure the greatest poet between-- between the war was a Jewish fellow named Julian Tuwim. And [? Sunwinski ?] and [? Juth. ?] And [? Manyareck, ?] and [? Sirbodiak ?] and mention you a dozen. In music, we have Rubinstein. Rubinstein was probably as much Polish, or more Polish, than I am. The only thing that Rubinstein was he was-- he was Jewish. But [INAUDIBLE] Paderewski, as a matter of fact, Paderewski was a teacher for Rubinstein. They accused Paderewski of being anti-semite. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So the-- you know, the anti-semitism is caused by the non-Jews and, quite often, by the Jews. In terms of Oskar Schindler, there are similarities between his helping Jews finding work for them and your assisting Jews in the same way.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, Schindler did it on a large scale. Why he did it is a question of interpretation. Because Schindler came to Poland with intention of making business. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He had the business, employed Jews, that were employed for many-- by many other companies.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a moment that-- that Schindler made-- probably Schindler probably saw the German atrocities. But the one that were there-- not everyone would tell you that, but I know some people-- he was a drunkard, liked women, made money hand in fist wherever he could. Then probably saw-- probably he was one of the Germans that saw that they're gonna lose it anyhow. But, as they say, you don't have to be a saint to be an angel, and this is the case with Schindler. He doesn't have to be a saint. But he did a great job, save so many Jews. And-- and Spielberg by making that movie did more then all of them together. All the movies together could not replace what-- what did because it's so real, and by-- by the-- and so good, that everybody want to see it. There-- I-- I didn't know of anyone that didn't see Jew or no Jew. And I think that in-- in-- in some way, large or small, did help subdue the anti-semitism.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Damski, you got a medal of honor, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: I got a medal from Yad Vashem.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yad Vashem. When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh my god.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have it written on my medal. Can I?

speaker: interviewer
text: Approximately?

speaker: interviewee
text: And--

speaker: interviewer
text: 10 years ago?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I got it quite late because I was-- I was asked to do submit my name but I always thought that--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you hold it for the camera also?

speaker: interviewee
text: --we should do it without any honors, but my mother-in-law wanted me always to-- to. And finally, I-- I got the medal. [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you hold it up for the camera?

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh-huh. There it is. The nice side.

speaker: interviewer
text: Turn it around.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you turn it around? There.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: And towards the light a little bit, in that direction? Yeah, there you go. Perfect.

speaker: interviewer
text: The other one.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and the other medal you're holding, what is-- what is that one?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Moral Courage Award, Jewish Foundation for Christian Rescuers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Moral Courage Award. Great. Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I-- two years ago, I became an honorary citizen of the state of Israel. It was a great honor for me. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was invited by a gentleman by the name Harvy Soner to Israel. He-- he sponsored about 400 rescuers. He sponsored a trip to Israel. And I was invited by him, and I went to Israel, where we had a meeting with the-- with the mayor of-- of Tel Aviv. I had some photographs with him. And it was an unforgettable trip. We were a group of [INAUDIBLE] 27 people of the same way of thinking. And we were discussing and-- to the point that even I didn't know it. But a year later, I found in the Polish newspapers from Newark-- from New York that-- about that trip. And whoever wrote it-- wrote it was such an interesting conversation that is too bad that it was not put on film or tape.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that-- I have to say a few words about Mr. Soner. He spent millions of dollars. When we went to Israel, we were treated like like royalty.

speaker: interviewee
text: That Soner, he's a little bit-- he has a little-- little trouble with his hand. But he was working every morning with a bag. He give us everybody 50, about 50 postcards that we could send to our families. He wouldn't let us to put stamps on it. Every morning, he went with his bag, collected that-- those pictures and sent them out.

speaker: interviewer
text: When we went to the-- to-- we went on the Via Dolorosa, went to a-- a stand with books. Whatever language about Jerusalem. I think about Holy Land. He said, who's Polish-- how many Poles, how many Hungarian, how many there were Romanians? There were five-- five or seven nationalities with us. He bought a book for everyone.

speaker: interviewee
text: He had to a piece of bread on his arm and liked it. And he says good bread, try it. Everybody liked it. [LAUGHS] It's-- an unbelievable man. It's-- it's-- And-- and-- and when we were in the Jerusalem, we went there when there was a 50th anniversary of the-- of the uprising in Warsaw. I believed it was. Or they get or some-- some-- some 50th anniversary. And I-- I was the one chosen to lay the wreath. There were over 100 different organizations from all over the world laying the wreaths in front of the monument of the Warsaw ghetto uprising.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then I was always-- also, I said, honored. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In-- in the Yad Vashem, there's that rotunda. Have you've been in Yad Vashem? And you know where you have to name of the-- of the concentration camps? There was a-- a ceremony, and I was the one that laid the wreath. So I was very honored with that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And Jerusalem-- [INAUDIBLE] is an unforgettable city. It's-- it's-- some Polish writer said that when you go to Jerusalem, you get Jerusalem syndrome. You want to go back. It's-- it's not like seeing Venice or Rome. Or-- it's something different. You-- the-- the dignity of the-- overwhelms you and you feel it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. Coming to the present time, your daughter is here, and we'll have her come on camera in a little while. But when was she born? Where was she born? She was born in Poland, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: My daughter was born in Poland, yes. In '45.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. And you have two grandchildren at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two grandchildren, yes. [? Taryn ?] and [? Ariel. ?] [? Taryn ?] [? Alanna, ?] [? Lisa ?] [? Ariel. ?] Very lovely children. They go to-- they both learn Hebrew. And I encourage them. I think it's very good. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I hope they grow up as good-- good people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we go to the photos, is there anything that you would like to add, anything that you would like to say that I haven't asked you about?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, just-- I think that-- I'd just like to thank you for spending the time in doing this. I think it's very essential for-- for the future because I do not believe-- though I-- I wish I'm wrong-- that anti-semitism will diminish. It-- it's there for so many thousand years, despite the fact that the Jews had so many achievements, contributed so much to our civilization, yet there's still anti-semitism.

speaker: interviewer
text: But perhaps [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] work done now by you [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] will probably spread around a little bit. It will be shown to people. People that-- that have nothing to do with Judaism will listen to people like myself, and start to think that all we need from people to think it over. Am I really right? What I'm doing when I'm an anti-semite. And it is-- the-- the-- I think this is the mission actually that is getting, people to think, what is it? Why-- why am I-- how can I be an anti-semite when I saw And I think, and this is the greatest value. Not that they're making millions on it, that is beside the point. Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you for everything. We would like to look at the photographs right now.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I [? bring them-- ?] There's a picture of my parents' wedding. It been taken in 1912. There's my parents in the middle. Next to them, my grandparents and my mother's brothers and her sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in Germany. In Hamburg, Dulsberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: In-- in where? I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Germany. Hamburg, Dulsberg is the name of place where the wedding took place.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us their full names of the people that you can identify?

speaker: interviewee
text: Adam Nowak. [? Franciszek ?] Nowak.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which ones? Which one is Adam?

speaker: interviewee
text: From the left.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: The smallest one.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: The next one is [? Franciszek ?] Nowak. He was later a lieutenant in the Polish Air Forces right after the war. Next is my grandmother, [? Tophlia ?] Gryszinska. Next, my mother, Stanislava Nowak. My father, John Nowak. My grandfather, Adam Nowak.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hold on.

speaker: interviewee
text: Franciszek Nowak. Pardon me. John Nowak, he was a [? male ?] in a small town in Poland. [? Anna ?] Nowak [? Soabadoska. ?] Next is [? Yusuf ?] Nowak. He also was a [? male ?] in a small town in Poland. This is my father, John Damski. Picture is taken in about 1921.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where? Where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- this picture was taken in Zabrze. It's a southeastern part of Poland. And this is my picture in the Polish Army, taken in [? town ?] [? Pidagros ?] in 1936.

speaker: interviewer
text: Approximately, how old were you here?

speaker: interviewee
text: 21. 22 almost.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your rank?

speaker: interviewee
text: My rank was, at this time, I was a-- plain solider. Later, I became a sergeant. When I-- This is a picture when I was a non-commissioned officers in [? Taposini ?] in 1934. I'm third from the right.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was taken in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Taken in Poland, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And these were your comrades?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were my colleagues in my-- in my squad. This is a picture taken in [INAUDIBLE] I believe in 1933 or early '34. I'm on the left side, my mother-in-law is in the middle, my aunt. On the right is my wife's former husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is '43, 1943, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you-- can you tell us their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: John Damski, Hennah Getla Rozen. And Maximilian Herdenhut.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is when you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Herdenhut was killed in t the one I was talking about.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Killed by a Gestapo agent.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was-- this was your future mother-in-law--

speaker: interviewee
text: My future mother-in-law died over here in Los Angeles. She was-- was with us, [INAUDIBLE] died in 1980. Just-- this is my-- picture of my wife and myself. Taken probably 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her name is? Her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Zophia ?] dressing up for the-- for the [? refska ?] later. Christine Damski and John Damski-- [? Jan ?] Damski at this time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in-- in-- near Warsaw when my brother used to live in there. We went once in awhile to-- for a few days to cool off.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was on holiday? A little bit of holiday?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was holiday most days. This is of-- a wedding picture of my daughter. On the left is I, myself, John Damski, my daughter, Eva, her husband, [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] [? Carl ?] Muchnick. And my wife, Christine Damski. It was taken at the temple--

speaker: interviewer
text: Stephen S. Wise?

speaker: interviewee
text: Stephen S. Wise Temple.

speaker: interviewer
text: In-- in Los Angeles?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Los Angeles.

speaker: interviewer
text: California?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1983. These are my two granddaughters. On the left is [? Taryn ?] [? Alanna. ?] And on the right, [? Lisa ?] [? Ariel. ?] Was taken in 1992 in Los Angeles. This is a picture taken at the consulate in Los Angeles. On the left is Counsel General [? Ronin. ?] I believe I pronounced it right. And I'm on the right side. I received a medal from Yad Vashem, and I'm holding the certificate. And it was in 1989.
